claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's get started. To determine your Child Benefit eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

**First, let me ask about your child:**

**How old is your child?** I need to know if they are:
- Under 16 years old
- Between 16-19 years old
- 20 years or older

This is important because:
- Children under 16 automatically qualify (subject to other conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for your help. I'm actually asking about two children. 

Alex is 16, and Blake is 5 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for both Alex (16 years old) and Blake (5 years old).

Let me work through each child:

**For Blake (5 years old):**
Since Blake is under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (5 years old):**
Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)? I assume at 5 years old, Blake is not working - is that correct?

**For Alex (16 years old):**
Now let me check Alex's situation. Since Alex is 16, there are additional requirements:

1. **First, is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?** Children aged 16+ who work 24 hours or more per week are not eligible unless they're in approved education or training.

2. **Is Alex enrolled in approved education or training?** This could include:
   - A-levels or similar qualifications
   - Scottish Highers
   - NVQs up to level 3
   - Home education (if started before age 16)
   - Unpaid work preparation or training courses

Please let me know about both children's work and education status.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Blake doesn't work at all - he's only 5!

As for Alex, no, he doesn't work. He's at sixth form college doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that's very helpful! Let me continue the assessment for both children.

**For Blake (5 years old):**
Great - Blake doesn't work. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake:** Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit? (I assume not at age 5, but I need to confirm)

**For Alex (16, doing A-levels at sixth form):**
Excellent! Since Alex is doing A-levels, he's in approved education. Now I need to check a few more things about Alex:

1. **Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

2. **Is Alex enrolled in an advanced apprenticeship** or higher apprenticeship?

Please let me know about these for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither Blake nor Alex receive any benefits in their own right. And no, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship - he's just doing his A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue checking the requirements for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex is doing A-levels (not an apprenticeship) and Blake is only 5, neither is in a disqualifying apprenticeship. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about **your residency status** (as the claimant). 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet ONE of these:

1. **Do you normally live in the UK?** (This is the most common situation)

2. Do you have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?

3. Do you have pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme AND:
   - Are you working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 months)?
   - OR actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)?
   - OR have sufficient resources to support yourself and your family (and not claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)?
   - OR are you studying with sufficient resources?
   - OR are you a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside?

4. Are you a Crown servant living abroad or living in certain qualifying countries?

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That satisfies the residency requirement. Now let me check the next requirement about your responsibility for the children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Blake and Alex.

**Are you responsible for both Blake and Alex?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of the following applies:

1. **Do you live with Blake and Alex?** (This is the most common situation)

2. Do you contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)?

3. Do you pay maintenance under a court order or agreement?

Do you live with both children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home, but Alex doesn't - he lives with his mum. But I do help out with Alex financially - I contribute towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me make sure I understand the situation correctly:

- **Blake (5 years old)**: Lives with you at home ✓
- **Alex (16 years old)**: Lives with his mum, but you contribute financially towards his upkeep

**For Alex specifically:** To be considered responsible through financial contributions, you need to pay **at least the same amount as Child Benefit** towards his care. This can include:
- Money for food, clothes, pocket money, housing costs
- Regular contributions or lump sum payments
- Presents and other support (equivalent value)

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about Alex:** 

Do you contribute **at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount** towards Alex's upkeep? 

The current Child Benefit rates are approximately:
- £25.60 per week for the eldest child
- £16.95 per week for additional children

So for Alex, if he's your only child for Child Benefit purposes, you'd need to contribute at least around £25.60 per week (about £110 per month). If Blake would be your eldest qualifying child, then for Alex you'd need to contribute at least around £16.95 per week (about £73 per month).

**Do your contributions meet or exceed these amounts?**

Also, one more important question: **Does Alex's mum currently claim Child Benefit for Alex?** This is crucial because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep - I help with clothes, school supplies, that sort of thing.

As for his mum, yes, she does claim Child Benefit for Alex already.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This is a crucial situation:

**The Key Issue:** Only **one person** can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. Since Alex's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you cannot also claim for him - even though you contribute financially to his upkeep.

Let me continue to check your eligibility for **Blake**, who lives with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, for **Blake (5 years old)** who lives with you:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?** 

- If no one else is claiming for Blake, we can continue with your eligibility assessment
- If someone else is claiming but you both agree you should claim instead, that can be arranged
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

What's the situation with Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Blake. It's just me and him at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue checking the requirements for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Blake's living situation:

**Which of the following describes Blake's current situation?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (most common)
2. In hospital
3. In local authority care
4. Being fostered

I assume Blake is living normally at home with you - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Blake lives at home with me, just a normal situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! One final question about Blake:

**Is the local council paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?** 

In other words, is the council providing any financial support for Blake's living costs or care? (This would generally disqualify you from Child Benefit)

I assume the answer is no, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Blake. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me get the final outcome for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me now provide you with the complete assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Final Result**

---

### **For Blake (5 years old): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Great news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake is 5 years old (under 16) ✓
- Blake does not work ✓
- Blake does not receive benefits in their own right ✓
- You live in the UK (Manchester) ✓
- Blake lives with you at home ✓
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake ✓
- The local council is not paying for Blake's care ✓

---

### **For Alex (16 years old): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you **cannot** claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Although Alex meets the age and education requirements (doing A-levels at sixth form) ✓
- Although you contribute financially to his upkeep (£50/week) ✓
- **Alex's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for him** ❌

**Important:** Under UK rules, **only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any time.** Since Alex's mum is already claiming for him, you cannot also claim.

---

### **Next Steps for Blake:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can apply online or by post using form CH2
2. **You'll need to provide:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Blake's birth certificate or adoption certificate
3. **Payments:** Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
4. **Important note:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (this doesn't affect eligibility, just means you may need to pay some back through tax)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

### **About Alex:**

If you and Alex's mother wanted to change who claims Child Benefit for Alex, she would need to stop her claim first, and then you could apply. However, this would need to be agreed between you both.

Do you have any questions about claiming for Blake or about the situation with Alex?

reasonings: Blake is 5 years old (under 16), does not work, does not receive benefits in their own right, is not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK (Manchester), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, Blake lives at home in a normal situation, and the local council is not paying for Blake's accommodation or maintenance. Blake meets all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements.

Alex is 16 years old and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college), does not work 24+ hours per week, does not receive benefits in his own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. You contribute £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold), demonstrating financial responsibility. However, Alex's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Under UK rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. Therefore, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex while his mother is already claiming it.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Manchester and meets UK residency requirements. They have two children: Blake (5) who lives with them, and Alex (16) who lives with his mother but for whom the claimant contributes £50/week. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, as all eligibility criteria are met and no one else is claiming for Blake. However, they cannot claim for Alex because Alex's mother already claims Child Benefit for him, and only one person can claim for the same child at any time.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Therefore, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex while his mother is already claiming it.
